ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for chicken teppanyaki; 'chicken dish' (FOODON_03302051) used as the closest parent match. No exact term for teppanyaki preparation exists in FoodOn as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKN (chicken meat, cooked) used as the closest parent match; no specific teppanyaki preparation code exists in FoodEx2. The code covers the primary ingredient and cooking state but not the specific teppanyaki method or soy sauce marinade.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of chicken teppanyaki is approximately 200 g, representing one restaurant-style plate with chicken, bean sprouts, and green onions.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken teppanyaki — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chicken teppanyaki (cr_id F11020023), confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate chicken dish with soy sauce marinade
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken teppanyaki. GI estimated at approximately 15 based on analogous low-carbohydrate cooked chicken dishes with soy-based marinades from the University of Sydney GI Database. Carbohydrate content is very low at 1.0 g/100g, yielding a negligible glycemic load of approximately 0.3 per 200 g serving. This is consistent with the GI profile of similar protein-dominant, low-carbohydrate Asian chicken preparations.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 548 mg per 100 g from dark soy sauce marinade
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 548 mg/100 g is elevated and a 200 g serving delivers approximately 1096 mg sodium — nearly half the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg — so individuals with hypertension should request low-sodium soy sauce, limit portion size to 100–120 g, and avoid adding extra condiments at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate at 1.0 g/100 g with high protein at 16.2 g/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 1.0 g carbohydrate and 0.8 g sugar per 100 g, chicken teppanyaki has a negligible glycemic impact and is suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes when paired with non-starchy vegetables rather than white rice, and when the marinade is confirmed to contain minimal added sugar or mirin.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (548 mg/100 g) offset by moderate total fat (8.8 g/100 g) and low saturated fat (~2.2 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The relatively low saturated fat content (~2.2 g/100 g) and high protein are favourable for cardiovascular health, but the high sodium from soy sauce marinade is a significant concern for blood pressure management; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose low-sodium soy sauce, limit portion to 100–150 g, and avoid additional salty condiments.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken teppanyaki is a Japanese-origin dish consisting of chicken meat with skin, bean sprouts, and green onions cooked on a hot iron plate (teppan) and seasoned with dark soy sauce. The teppanyaki cooking method involves high-heat searing on a flat iron griddle, producing a lightly charred exterior with a juicy interior and a savoury, umami-rich flavour profile. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served as a hawker centre or food court item, often accompanied by steamed white rice. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 148 kcal, 16.2 g protein, 8.8 g fat (approximately 2.2 g saturated), 1.0 g carbohydrate, 0.8 g sugar, 1.8 g dietary fibre, and 548 mg sodium. The high protein content makes it suitable for muscle maintenance, while the very low carbohydrate content supports glycaemic stability. However, the elevated sodium from dark soy sauce is a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker contexts. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. The bean sprouts and green onions contribute modest fibre and micronutrients including calcium (31 mg/100 g) and iron (1.6 mg/100 g).
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken teppanyaki?
Is chicken teppanyaki suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database canonical entry for Chicken teppanyaki (cr_id F11020023) with a confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent.
notes: Saturated fat estimated at approximately 2.2 g/100 g based on typical skin-on chicken fat composition from USDA FoodData Central (chicken, broiler, meat and skin, cooked, roasted — USDA SR Legacy item 05013); this field is not explicitly reported in the HPB source entry. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish and detailed micronutrient and fatty acid fractionation data for this specific Singapore hawker preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA as of 2026. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for chicken teppanyaki exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The input record incorrectly tagged 'plant_based' as a dietary tag — this has been removed as the dish contains chicken. The input health_suitability entries for diabetes incorrectly set suitable=false despite the nutritional profile being favourable for diabetes; these have been corrected in health_context. Halal status retained from input dietary_tags as consistent with typical Singapore hawker preparation. Ontology match types corrected to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 to comply with the allowed enumeration values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].

Updated gap notes to reflect closest_parent rationale. All other sections preserved from iteration 2.
